records.ety: [Probably fr. OE. stightlen, sti<?/tlen, to dispose, arrange, govern, freq. of stihten, AS. stihtan: cf. G. stiften to found, to establish.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Stickled (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Stickling.]

quotes: When he [the angel] sees half of the Christians killed, and the rest in a fair way of being routed, he stickles betwixt the remainder of God's host and the race of fiends.

defs: To contend, contest, or altercate, esp. in a pertinacious manner on insufficient grounds.
quotes: Fortune, as she 's wont, turned fickle, And for the foe began to stickle.
While for paltry punk they roar and stickle.
The obstinacy with which he stickles for the wrong.

defs: To play fast and loose; to pass from one side to the other; to trim.
To separate, as combatants; hence, to quiet, to appease, as disputants.
quotes: Which [question] violently they pursue, Nor stickled would they be.

defs: To intervene in; to stop, or put an end to, by intervening; hence, to arbitrate.
quotes: They ran to him, and, pulling him back by force, stickled that unnatural fray.

defs: A shallow rapid in a river; also, the current below a waterfall.
quotes: Patient anglers, standing all the day Near to some shallow stickle or deep bay.
